items.q: Does the binary number 10 mean ten?
items.correct: No - in binary, 10 means one two and zero units, which is the denary value two; the same symbols mean different values in different bases.
items.misconception: Yes - 10 is ten no matter what base it is written in.
items.why: Digits carry their denary meanings into binary by habit. Reading binary aloud as 'one-zero' rather than 'ten' breaks the reflex and forces place-value thinking.

items.q: Is the largest value in a byte 256?
items.correct: No - a byte has 256 different patterns, but they run from 0 to 255, so the largest value is 255.
items.misconception: Yes - eight bits count up to exactly 256.
items.why: The count of patterns and the largest value differ by one because zero takes a pattern. This off-by-one trap appears constantly in capacity questions.

items.q: What does the hexadecimal digit A represent in denary?
items.correct: 10 - the letters A to F continue counting after 9, representing denary 10 to 15.
items.misconception: 1 - A is the first letter so it stands for one.
items.random_wrong: 65 - A is always its ASCII code.
items.why: Alphabet position and ASCII both interfere. Hex letters simply extend the digit set: A=10, B=11 ... F=15, allowing sixteen symbols per place.

items.q: How many bits make one byte?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 8. “16” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: 4 bits - half a byte. “2 bits” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which symbols are valid hexadecimal digits?
items.why: Correct answer: 0-9 and A-F. “0-9 only” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The largest denary value that can be stored in one byte is:
items.why: Correct answer: 255. “256” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What is the binary number 1011 in denary?
items.why: The real figure is 11. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
